=== CITED COMMUNITY REPORTS ===
REPORT 11 [resolved-as: community_report]:
# Scan-to-Mail Community — SMTP Relay, Multifunction Devices, Printer VLAN

This community centers on the Scan-to-Mail service implemented by multifunction printers that send scanned documents via a dedicated SMTP RELAY. The SMTP RELAY enforces an allow-list of source IP ranges (printer VLAN) and a maximum message-size limit (recently reduced to ~10 MB), and it requires authentication by a SERVICE ACCOUNT and TLS. Large, multi-page high-resolution scans (default 600 dpi, PDF compression disabled) exceeded the relay limit and were rejected with explicit log entries ("MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT" / "Relay denied"). Internal routing allowed delivery to internal mailboxes, but external forwarding failed until the MAIL TEAM updated the relay allow-list for the printer VLAN and moderately increased the size limit while support adjusted device scan profiles (reduced to 300 dpi and enabled compression). Troubleshooting and remediation required coordinated activity across Support (ticket owners and engineers), Network, and Mail teams and included temporary role/MFA changes for the service account to complete connector operations and testing to external recipients (Gmail, partner). [Data: Entities (136,656,659,135,658), Relationships (214,1135,1140,1128,1139)]

## SMTP RELAY is the single choke point controlling external Scan-to-Mail delivery

The dedicated SMTP RELAY is the central infrastructure component that authenticates devices, enforces TLS, validates source IP ranges, and applies a maximum message-size limit; oversized messages and unallowed source IP ranges caused outbound scan deliveries to be rejected at this component. This is documented in the relay description and was the primary functional bottleneck during incidents. [Data: Entities (136,658), Relationships (1135,1140,214)]

Because the relay sits between multifunction devices and external recipients, changes to its policy (for spam mitigation or allow‑lists) directly affect business workflows like Scan-to-Mail; this dependency was confirmed by successful post‑remediation tests to external providers (Gmail, partner) after relay configuration changes. Operational ownership of these controls rests with the MAIL TEAM, who implemented and later adjusted the size limit and allow-list. [Data: Entities (142,136,143,144), Relationships (1142,218,219)]

## Relay log entries provided clear, reproducible diagnostics that localized failures to the SMTP RELAY

Support analysis identified specific SMTP log entries such as "MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT" which explicitly indicate that the relay rejected oversized messages rather than the internal mail server or printer device failing silently. These log entries were used as the primary diagnostic evidence during investigation. [Data: Entities (663,657), Relationships (1139,1146)]

Internal mail server logs corroborated the diagnosis by showing no incoming messages for the failed scans, which demonstrates that the relay rejection occurred before forwarding to internal mail infrastructure. This separation is important operationally because it focuses remediation on relay policy and message sizing rather than internal mailbox processing. [Data: Entities (654), Relationships (1138)]

## Printer VLAN allow-list misconfiguration blocked external delivery while internal delivery remained functional

The multifunction device was placed in a newly defined PRINTER VLAN; the SMTP RELAY’s allow-list did not include that VLAN’s address range, producing "Relay denied" behavior for external messages even though internal routing allowed local delivery. This explains the symptom where the device indicated "Send successful" but external recipients never received messages. [Data: Entities (135,662,136), Relationships (210,214,1150)]

Remediation required updating the relay configuration to include the printer VLAN so devices could be accepted as valid submitters for outbound relay. This change underlines a recurring operational risk when VLAN or IP reassignments occur without parallel relay policy updates. [Data: Relationships (214)]

## Device scan profile (resolution/compression) was a root contributor to oversized messages; profile changes restored large-scan delivery

The devices used a default scan profile of 600 dpi with PDF compression disabled, producing large multi‑page PDFs that exceeded the relay’s 10 MB limit; reducing resolution to 300 dpi and enabling PDF compression produced smaller files that successfully transmitted in tests. This confirms that both infrastructure limits and endpoint behavior jointly determine Scan-to-Mail reliability. [Data: Entities (659,660,661,655), Relationships (1147,1148,1149,1130)]

Operationally, this means remediation may be twofold: (a) adjust relay size policy to tolerate legitimate business use-cases, and (b) standardize device scan profiles (e.g., lower default dpi, enable compression) in the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard to limit recurrence. The ticket records show support executed device changes and tests (one‑page and 25‑page scans) to validate the fix. [Data: Relationships (1128,1130)]

## Remediation required cross-team coordination and temporary privilege/MFA changes for service account operations

Resolution involved coordinated activity between Support (ticket owners and engineers), Network, and the MAIL TEAM to update relay allow-lists and size limits, as well as device profile changes and testing to external addresses. The tickets (TI-1624175, TI-1624682) document these coordinated steps and closure notes citing adherence to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. [Data: Entities (132,652,142), Relationships (197,1123,1142)]

To perform connector reinstallation/operation one time, the SERVICE ACCOUNT was temporarily granted the INTUNE ADMIN ROLE and excluded from MFA, indicating operational shortcuts required to complete changes; these actions were reversed after remediation, but they represent an elevated-privilege path that needs governance. [Data: Entities (106,645), Relationships (1120,1119)]

## Device feedback ("Send successful") is misleading without relay/queue visibility and contributed to delayed detection

Multifunction devices reported a local confirmation message ("Send successful") immediately after submission, but the SMTP RELAY later rejected large messages; the device confirmation thus gave a false positive, delaying detection until logs or recipient complaints surfaced. Support notes explicitly document this disparity. [Data: Entities (662,663), Relationships (1150,1139)]

Operational runbooks should require immediate mail-queue and relay‑log checks when devices report success but recipients report non‑receipt, because endpoint success state does not guarantee end-to-end delivery in relay‑mediated flows. [Data: Entities (727), Relationships (1307)]

## DNS/TLS and external mail security changes were investigated as potential contributors and should be part of pre-change impact assessments

Customer-reported changes to DNS forwarding and an external mail security solution were reviewed during the investigation to ensure they did not cause the failure; TLS configuration is also required for Scan-to-Mail connections. These elements can interact with relay behavior or external filtering and therefore merit inclusion in change impact analysis for mail and scanning infrastructure. [Data: Entities (138,139,725), Relationships (223,1305)]

Including DNS/TLS and external mail-security checks in the Print & Scanning change checklist reduces the chance that unrelated changes will cause delivery regressions; tickets reference these checks in their investigation steps. [Data: Relationships (223,1305,1146)]

## Operational and security gap: relay policy changes for spam control vs. business needs require formal governance and monitoring

The MAIL TEAM reduced the maximum email size as a spam mitigation step and later had to moderate the limit to restore business scanning functionality. While anti‑spam controls are important, the unilateral application of a global size limit without exception handling for sanctioned device classes (printer VLAN / Scan-to-Mail) created an availability impact to business workflows. The ticket trail records the initial reduction and the later coordination to adjust the limit. [Data: Entities (664,658,142), Relationships (1142,1135)]

Additionally, temporary elevation of service account privileges and exclusion from MFA created short-term compliance exposures; these actions were necessary to complete connector operations but underscore the need for documented privileged-operation runbooks, time-bound approvals, logging, and post‑change verification to avoid policy drift. Implementing exception handling for sanctioned devices and alerting on relay rejection patterns will reduce both risk and time to remediation. [Data: Entities (106,645), Relationships (1120,1119)]
is_rec_reason: This statement reports documented remediations that were actually performed (past actions validated in records) and does not propose or recommend any future action.
groundedness_reason: Ticket records and the community report explicitly document reducing device resolution to 300 dpi with PDF compression enabled, moderately increasing the SMTP relay size limit, updating the relay allow‑list to include the printer VLAN, and successful tests to external addresses (Gmail/partner).

# Azure AD Connect / Microsoft Graph group-sync: Engineer-led RCA and remediation

The available records describe a single, focused post-incident analysis where SYSTEM ENGINEER 14 took ownership of a group-membership synchronization issue between on-premises AD and Azure AD. The engineer used Azure AD Connect synchronization context to reproduce and explain the problem and relied on Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate and compare cloud-native and synchronized on-prem group membership. The engineer's work included in-depth analysis, reproduction in test, identification of root cause, and guidance on remediation actions described as consolidation, cleanup and a full sync.

## Clear primary analyst: SYSTEM ENGINEER 14 led the investigation and remediation

SYSTEM ENGINEER 14 is identified as the engineer who took over the ticket for in‑depth analysis, reproduced the issue in test, identified the root cause, and guided remediation actions (consolidation, cleanup, full sync).

Synchronized On-Prem group:

REPORT 33 [resolved-as: community_report]:
# Customer Company 13 — SAP Print Paths & Customer Contacts

This community centers on CUSTOMER COMPANY 13 and its business‑critical SAP print paths, with a small set of named customer contacts (CONTACT PERSON 12, Tom / ContactPerson13@kf13.com) participating in incident confirmation and ticket correspondence. CUSTOMER COMPANY 13 is the operational owner for SAP print routing and has declared SAP print paths high priority; customer contacts served as primary responders and authoritative confirmation sources during the support incident. Internal/external handoffs are visible in the ticket thread (Employee 18 checking status with CONTACT PERSON 12), and a single customer email address is used for acknowledgements and feedback. The dataset documents ownership, the business criticality of printing, direct communication links used during the incident, and resolution confirmation workflow.

## Clear operational ownership: CUSTOMER COMPANY 13 owns SAP print paths

The dataset explicitly identifies CUSTOMER COMPANY 13 as the owner and operational boundary for SAP print paths and notes those paths are classified as business‑critical and must be prioritized when disrupted, which is a strong indicator that printing is a production service requiring SLAs and prioritized response [Data: Entities (148), Relationships (231)].

This single ownership attribution means architecture, change control, and operational responsibility for SAP-to-Windows print routing reside with the customer. Any architecture or runbook updates should therefore align with CUSTOMER COMPANY 13’s operational policy and change windows, and vendor/partner teams should treat print path incidents as high priority per the declared classification [Data: Relationships (231)].

## SAP print paths are explicitly business‑critical

The SAP print paths entity is defined in the dataset as the logical and network routes from SAP through spool/output management to Windows print queues and physical printers, and is classified as business‑critical requiring priority handling when disrupted [Data: Entities (163), Relationships (231)].

This semantic classification should drive monitoring, alerting thresholds, and runbook priorities: print spooler health, SAP output queues, network connectivity to print servers, and Windows print server queue depth are appropriate telemetry sources to include in an elevated monitoring tier tied to incident response procedures [Data: Entities (163), Relationships (231)].

## Small set of named customer stakeholders acted as primary responders and confirmation sources

CONTACT PERSON 12 is recorded as the customer‑side contact and Head of Customer Services and served as the primary responder in the ticket thread; they reported that printing was initially not working and later confirmed the issue was resolved—positioning them as the authoritative confirmation source for remediation [Data: Entities (513)].

Additionally, Tom (Head of IT Switzerland Informatics) used the email contact ContactPerson13@kf13.com to send acknowledgements and feedback, indicating a lightweight but consistent customer escalation and acknowledgement channel [Data: Entities (514), Entities (529), Relationships (875), Relationships (873)].

## Documented operational handoffs and status checks during the incident

The record shows Employee 18 corresponded directly with CONTACT PERSON 12 to check whether the issue had been resolved and to gather status updates during troubleshooting, demonstrating an explicit operational handoff and verification step in the incident lifecycle [Data: Relationships (874), Entities (513)].

That correspondence provides a concrete escalation/confirmation pathway: technical responders escalate status to a named customer contact (CONTACT PERSON 12), who then confirms remediation—useful for formalizing an SLA‑aligned closure procedure and audit trail [Data: Relationships (874), Entities (513)].

## Single customer email and small contact list create potential single points for confirmation/communication

A single customer email address (ContactPerson13@kf13.com) is used in the ticket thread for acknowledgements and feedback and is associated with Tom, the Head of IT Switzerland Informatics [Data: Entities (529), Entities (514), Relationships (875), Relationships (873)].

Relying on one primary address and a small roster of named contacts for incident confirmation creates risks if those individuals are unavailable (out of office, on leave, or unreachable). Operational resiliency would benefit from documented secondary contacts and a defined escalation matrix to avoid delays in confirmation and closure [Data: Entities (529), Relationships (873, 875)].

## Evidence supports establishing a simple, prioritized runbook for SAP printing incidents

Given the declared business criticality of SAP print paths and the ticket evidence of a troubleshooting/dialogue pattern (technical staff -> Contact Person 12 -> confirmation), there is a clear basis to codify a runbook that defines detection, owner notification, customer confirmation, and closure criteria [Data: Entities (163), Relationships (231), Relationships (874)].

A prioritized runbook should include: monitoring thresholds for SAP spool queues and Windows print servers; primary and secondary customer contacts; escalation timelines; temporary mitigation (e.g., alternate print routes or manual output extraction) and formal customer confirmation steps (which in this dataset were performed by CONTACT PERSON 12) to ensure auditable closure [Data: Entities (163), Entities (513), Relationships (874)].

## Recommended governance controls and near‑term actions

The records justify a focused set of governance actions: formalize CUSTOMER COMPANY 13 ownership in a shared ops playbook, document the Contact Person 12 confirmation step in closure criteria, add at least one secondary contact to the ContactPerson13@kf13.com point of contact, and create a prioritized monitoring and alerting policy for SAP print paths per their PRIO HIGH classification [Data: Entities (148), Entities (513), Entities (529), Relationships (231), Relationships (873)].

These actions are low‑effort, high‑impact: they align documented responsibilities to the ownership already stated in the dataset, reduce single‑point communication risk observed in the ticket, and translate the business‑critical classification into operational controls that can be audited and exercised during incident drills [Data: Relationships (231), Relationships (874), Entities (513)].

REPORT 64 [resolved-as: community_report]:
# Azure AD Connect vs Cloud COMPLIANCE_TEAM — External Guest Access Loss (SharePoint)

This community centers on a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group and an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group that share a display name but have distinct ObjectIDs. Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)]

## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership

Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)]

The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)]

## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected

External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)]

Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)]

## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection

A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)]

The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)]

## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency

SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)]

This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)]

## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode

There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)]

Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)]

## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings

The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)]

While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)]

## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules

The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)]

Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The evidence of an operational incident plus lack of documented governance in the dataset supports elevating this as a priority for follow-up. [Data: Relationships (435,437,446)]

general info & description
Print Services for SAP Output Standard
Version 1.0
Date: 25.07.2024
Status: Approved
This document describes the standard for the provisioning of SAP outputs (e.g., delivery notes, pick lists, labels) via central Windows print servers at Customer Company 13. The objective is a stable operation of SAP output processes, as printing disruptions can have direct impact on logistics and deliveries.
The standard covers the technical provisioning of SAP-relevant print queues on dedicated print servers as well as the operational guidelines following Windows updates, restores, or maintenance windows. Special focus is placed on the consistency of printer queue configuration (Queue Properties) and network sharing and publishing mechanisms, as these may be relevant for SAP print paths and spool processing.
Typical symptoms of deviations from this standard include: print jobs remaining stuck in SAP, print jobs disappearing from the queue without printing, or print jobs accumulating on the print server and only being released after manual intervention. This pattern was observed in ticket TI-931293 after a Windows update and subsequent VM restore.
design
Architecture overview
SAP System
→ SAP Spool / Output Management
→ SAP print target (logical printer definition)
→ Windows Printserver (SAP Printserver)
→ Print queues
→ Physical printers
Roles and components
1. SAP Output Management
Generates print jobs and transfers them to defined print targets. The availability and correct processing of print targets is business-critical.
2. SAP Printserver (Windows)
Serves as the central processing instance for SAP printing. The relevant queues are operated on dedicated print servers (in the ticket: VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04).
3. Print queues
Queue properties must be standardized and consistent. After system changes (updates, restores), it must be ensured that publishing/sharing parameters and queue status are correct and that no unintended states (e.g., stuck jobs) exist.
4. Print Spooler Service
The service is responsible for processing print jobs. A restart may temporarily influence symptoms but does not necessarily resolve configuration deviations at queue level.
Operational principles
• After Windows updates or VM restores, a validation of the SAP print servers must be performed (queue status, spooler, sharing/publishing of queues, test print from SAP).
• SAP print paths must be treated as business-critical; disruptions are to be handled with priority (classified as PRIO HIGH in the ticket).
• If printing issues occur after update/restore, queue configuration must be treated as the primary checkpoint, not only the spooler service.
decision tree & use cases
Decision Tree
Initial question: Does the issue affect only printing from SAP or also normal printing (e.g., Office)?
A) Only SAP printing affected, normal printing works
A1: Are all SAP print targets affected or only specific print servers?
• Only specific print servers affected → Perform SAP printserver-specific checks (queue status, publishing/sharing, spooler, restore state)
• All print servers affected → Check SAP output configuration (print targets, spool queue, SAP-side errors)
A2: Was there a Windows update or VM restore within the last 24–72 hours?
• Yes → Perform post-change validation according to standard (queue properties, sharing/publishing, test print)
• No → Proceed to A3
A3: Are SAP print jobs visible on the print server (queue) or do they remain stuck in SAP?
• Remain stuck in SAP → Check SAP output / connection to print target
• Visible on the print server but no print output → Check queue/spooler/sharing mechanism
B) SAP printing and normal printing affected
B1: Is the Print Spooler service running?
• No → Start service / check service recovery
• Yes → Proceed to B2
B2: Are queues paused or are jobs permanently stuck?
• Yes → Correct queue status, clear stuck jobs, then perform test print
• No → Check network path / printer reachability
C) Issue occurs after update/restore and is printserver-specific
C1: Check queue properties according to standard
• Validate publishing/sharing status of printer queues
• If inconsistent state: disable/enable relevant queue parameters as a reset measure
This procedure corresponds to the pattern documented in the ticket, where after update and restore the network sharing/queue functionality had to be corrected.

groundedness_reason: The mapping chain (SAP spool → print target → Windows queue) and that disabling/re‑enabling the network share after a VM restore cleared queued SAP documents are documented in the ticket and standard, but the answer's explicit reference to an 'SMB share' and the admonition to only apply the pattern if you have host privileges are not explicitly stated in the provided CONTEXT.

# Consulting Sample Company AG — Ticketing, BU Customer Services Run, Core Engineering (TI-931293)

## TI-931293: high-priority printing outage caused by Windows update + VM restore and resolved by toggling print-server network share

TI-931293 is documented as a PRIO HIGH ticket for Customer Company 13, opened after a phone report and created via the CRM/web portal workflow; it blocked SAP printing and deliveries until resolved [Data: Entities (149,531,530); Relationships (869,931)].

Root-cause context in the ticket notes links the failure mode to a Windows update followed by a VM restore, producing classic symptoms (stuck SAP jobs, disappearing jobs, or queues accumulating jobs) and requiring manual intervention on the print server queues [Data: Entities (149); Relationships (232)].

Employee 17 (Senior System Engineer in Core Engineering) identified the cause and performed remediation by disabling and re-enabling the network share on the print server queues, authored the resolution notes, and closed the ticket—demonstrating that Core Engineering performed hands‑on recovery for this incident class [Data: Entities (515,520,149); Relationships (877)].

## TI-931293 is used as an exemplar to justify PRIO HIGH classification for similar incidents

The ticket record and associated internal documentation explicitly cite TI-931293 as an example case of post-update printing failures and as justification for classifying similar incidents as PRIO HIGH, indicating organizational recognition of this recurring failure mode and its business impact [Data: Entities (149); Relationships (232)].

Because the ticket blocked order printing and deliveries, the example-driven classification suggests that the organization has started to codify severity rules for this incident type, which is valuable for triage and SLA alignment but also implies a need for preventative controls and runbooks to reduce recurrence [Data: Entities (149); Relationships (232)].

## Responsibility and escalation ownership is detectable but could be formalized into a runbook

Records show CRM handles intake and initial classification, BU Customer Services Run receives operational ownership and notifications, and Core Engineering (Employee 17) performs technical remediation—this establishes a de facto escalation path across three functional actors [Data: Entities (531,6,520,515); Relationships (931,882,25,877)].

However, the observed remediation (manual network-share toggle on print queues by Core Engineering) appears reactive and performed ad-hoc for this incident; the fact TI-931293 is cited as an exemplar suggests the practice may be repeated rather than automated, so a formal, documented runbook for post-update/VM-restore printing recovery would reduce time-to-resolution and reliance on individual engineers [Data: Entities (149,515,520); Relationships (232,877)].

## This incident class poses a material business-continuity risk for affected customers

TI-931293 directly prevented order printing from SAP and thereby blocked deliveries—an impact that crosses from IT operations into supply/logistics and customer fulfillment, which elevates severity beyond a local print issue [Data: Entities (149)].

Because the root cause is associated with Windows updates and VM restores, these events are likely to occur as part of normal maintenance and disaster recovery activities; without preventive controls, the organization risks repeated disruption of printing-dependent business processes when those maintenance activities occur [Data: Entities (149); Relationships (232)].

## Ticketing and notification tooling are integrated but rely on no-reply addresses and public website signatures for contact context

The ticket system publishes automated assignment/status messages via a NO-REPLY system account and a no-reply/support SMTP address (BU_CUSTOMER_SERVICES_RUN@) that are visible in the logs, indicating automated lifecycle notifications are generated but may not be monitored as actively as operator mailboxes [Data: Entities (618,533); Relationships (1049,866)].

## Actionable priorities: formalize runbook, monitor post-update VM restores, and align notifications/escalation SLAs

Because the incident was resolved by Core Engineering performing a targeted technical action (network-share toggle) and TI-931293 is explicitly used as an exemplar, the highest-impact actions are: 1) capture the remediation steps into a formal runbook and playbook for post-update/VM-restore printing failures; 2) implement post-patch/restore validation checks for print services and automated alerts for queue anomalies; and 3) codify assignment + escalation SLAs between CRM, BU Customer Services Run, and Core Engineering to ensure timely handoffs [Data: Entities (149,515,520,531,6); Relationships (232,877,882,869)].

general info & description
Print Services for SAP Output Standard
Version 1.0
Date: 25.07.2024
Status: Approved
This document describes the standard for the provisioning of SAP outputs (e.g., delivery notes, pick lists, labels) via central Windows print servers at Customer Company 13. The objective is a stable operation of SAP output processes, as printing disruptions can have direct impact on logistics and deliveries.
The standard covers the technical provisioning of SAP-relevant print queues on dedicated print servers as well as the operational guidelines following Windows updates, restores, or maintenance windows. Special focus is placed on the consistency of printer queue configuration (Queue Properties) and network sharing and publishing mechanisms, as these may be relevant for SAP print paths and spool processing.
Typical symptoms of deviations from this standard include: print jobs remaining stuck in SAP, print jobs disappearing from the queue without printing, or print jobs accumulating on the print server and only being released after manual intervention. This pattern was observed in ticket TI-931293 after a Windows update and subsequent VM restore.
design
Architecture overview
SAP System
→ SAP Spool / Output Management
→ SAP print target (logical printer definition)
→ Windows Printserver (SAP Printserver)
→ Print queues
→ Physical printers
Roles and components
1. SAP Output Management
Generates print jobs and transfers them to defined print targets. The availability and correct processing of print targets is business-critical.
2. SAP Printserver (Windows)
Serves as the central processing instance for SAP printing. The relevant queues are operated on dedicated print servers (in the ticket: VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04).
3. Print queues
Queue properties must be standardized and consistent. After system changes (updates, restores), it must be ensured that publishing/sharing parameters and queue status are correct and that no unintended states (e.g., stuck jobs) exist.
4. Print Spooler Service
The service is responsible for processing print jobs. A restart may temporarily influence symptoms but does not necessarily resolve configuration deviations at queue level.
Operational principles
• After Windows updates or VM restores, a validation of the SAP print servers must be performed (queue status, spooler, sharing/publishing of queues, test print from SAP).
• SAP print paths must be treated as business-critical; disruptions are to be handled with priority (classified as PRIO HIGH in the ticket).
• If printing issues occur after update/restore, queue configuration must be treated as the primary checkpoint, not only the spooler service.
decision tree & use cases
Decision Tree
Initial question: Does the issue affect only printing from SAP or also normal printing (e.g., Office)?
A) Only SAP printing affected, normal printing works
A1: Are all SAP print targets affected or only specific print servers?
• Only specific print servers affected → Perform SAP printserver-specific checks (queue status, publishing/sharing, spooler, restore state)
• All print servers affected → Check SAP output configuration (print targets, spool queue, SAP-side errors)
A2: Was there a Windows update or VM restore within the last 24–72 hours?
• Yes → Perform post-change validation according to standard (queue properties, sharing/publishing, test print)
• No → Proceed to A3
A3: Are SAP print jobs visible on the print server (queue) or do they remain stuck in SAP?
• Remain stuck in SAP → Check SAP output / connection to print target
• Visible on the print server but no print output → Check queue/spooler/sharing mechanism
B) SAP printing and normal printing affected
B1: Is the Print Spooler service running?
• No → Start service / check service recovery
• Yes → Proceed to B2
B2: Are queues paused or are jobs permanently stuck?
• Yes → Correct queue status, clear stuck jobs, then perform test print
• No → Check network path / printer reachability
C) Issue occurs after update/restore and is printserver-specific
C1: Check queue properties according to standard
• Validate publishing/sharing status of printer queues
• If inconsistent state: disable/enable relevant queue parameters as a reset measure
This procedure corresponds to the pattern documented in the ticket, where after update and restore the network sharing/queue functionality had to be corrected.

general info & description
Print Services for SAP Output Standard
Version 1.0
Date: 25.07.2024
Status: Approved
This document describes the standard for the provisioning of SAP outputs (e.g., delivery notes, pick lists, labels) via central Windows print servers at Customer Company 13. The objective is a stable operation of SAP output processes, as printing disruptions can have direct impact on logistics and deliveries.
The standard covers the technical provisioning of SAP-relevant print queues on dedicated print servers as well as the operational guidelines following Windows updates, restores, or maintenance windows. Special focus is placed on the consistency of printer queue configuration (Queue Properties) and network sharing and publishing mechanisms, as these may be relevant for SAP print paths and spool processing.
Typical symptoms of deviations from this standard include: print jobs remaining stuck in SAP, print jobs disappearing from the queue without printing, or print jobs accumulating on the print server and only being released after manual intervention. This pattern was observed in ticket TI-931293 after a Windows update and subsequent VM restore.
design
Architecture overview
SAP System
→ SAP Spool / Output Management
→ SAP print target (logical printer definition)
→ Windows Printserver (SAP Printserver)
→ Print queues
→ Physical printers
Roles and components
1. SAP Output Management
Generates print jobs and transfers them to defined print targets. The availability and correct processing of print targets is business-critical.
2. SAP Printserver (Windows)
Serves as the central processing instance for SAP printing. The relevant queues are operated on dedicated print servers (in the ticket: VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04).
3. Print queues
Queue properties must be standardized and consistent. After system changes (updates, restores), it must be ensured that publishing/sharing parameters and queue status are correct and that no unintended states (e.g., stuck jobs) exist.
4. Print Spooler Service
The service is responsible for processing print jobs. A restart may temporarily influence symptoms but does not necessarily resolve configuration deviations at queue level.
Operational principles
• After Windows updates or VM restores, a validation of the SAP print servers must be performed (queue status, spooler, sharing/publishing of queues, test print from SAP).
• SAP print paths must be treated as business-critical; disruptions are to be handled with priority (classified as PRIO HIGH in the ticket).
• If printing issues occur after update/restore, queue configuration must be treated as the primary checkpoint, not only the spooler service.
decision tree & use cases
Decision Tree
Initial question: Does the issue affect only printing from SAP or also normal printing (e.g., Office)?
A) Only SAP printing affected, normal printing works
A1: Are all SAP print targets affected or only specific print servers?
• Only specific print servers affected → Perform SAP printserver-specific checks (queue status, publishing/sharing, spooler, restore state)
• All print servers affected → Check SAP output configuration (print targets, spool queue, SAP-side errors)
A2: Was there a Windows update or VM restore within the last 24–72 hours?
• Yes → Perform post-change validation according to standard (queue properties, sharing/publishing, test print)
• No → Proceed to A3
A3: Are SAP print jobs visible on the print server (queue) or do they remain stuck in SAP?
• Remain stuck in SAP → Check SAP output / connection to print target
• Visible on the print server but no print output → Check queue/spooler/sharing mechanism
B) SAP printing and normal printing affected
B1: Is the Print Spooler service running?
• No → Start service / check service recovery
• Yes → Proceed to B2
B2: Are queues paused or are jobs permanently stuck?
• Yes → Correct queue status, clear stuck jobs, then perform test print
• No → Check network path / printer reachability
C) Issue occurs after update/restore and is printserver-specific
C1: Check queue properties according to standard
• Validate publishing/sharing status of printer queues
• If inconsistent state: disable/enable relevant queue parameters as a reset measure
This procedure corresponds to the pattern documented in the ticket, where after update and restore the network sharing/queue functionality had to be corrected.

﻿general info & description
Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Customer Company
Version: 1.0
Date: 15.10.2025
Author: IT Infrastructure
Status: Approved
This document describes the standard for the print and scan infrastructure within the customer company.
The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles:
• Central printer management via print servers
• Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO)
• Standardized driver versions
• Location-based printer concepts
• Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail
The print infrastructure is based on:
• Windows print servers
• Location-based printer queues
• User- or group-based assignment
• VLAN-separated printer networks
Manual installation of local printers is not intended.
design
Architecture overview
User device
→ Print server
→ Printer (in printer VLAN)
Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server.
Printer provisioning
Printers are:
• Created as a queue on the print server
• Configured with a standardized driver
• Assigned via GPO based on location or department
Default printers are set automatically.
Driver standard
Only approved and tested driver versions are used.
After Windows updates, it must be verified:
• Whether drivers remain compatible
• Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active
• Whether printers are still mapped correctly
Scan-to-Mail
Scan-to-Mail is performed:
• Via a dedicated SMTP relay
• Using a configured service account
• Secured with TLS
Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality.
Location concept
Each location has:
• At least one default printer
• One multifunction printer with scan capability
• Assignment via AD groups
Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN.
 
decision tree & use cases
Decision Tree
Initial question:
Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning?
A) Printing affected
A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”?
• No →
Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group.
If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied.
If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update.
If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server.
• Yes →
Proceed to A2.
A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue?
• No →
Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment).
• Yes →
Proceed to A3.
A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue?
• Yes →
Check whether the queue is paused.
If not → Check the Print Spooler service.
If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN).
• No →
Proceed to A4.
A4: Does the issue affect only one location?
• Yes →
Check VLAN reachability.
Check DNS resolution of the printer.
Check location-based GPO assignment.
• No →
Check driver compatibility or print server service.
A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update?
• Yes →
Check driver compatibility.
Check Point-and-Print policy.
Validate security policies.
• No →
Further analysis according to standard process.
B) Scanning affected
B1: Is printing still working?
• Yes →
Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path.
• No →
First analyze printing issue according to section A.
B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning?
• SMTP or connection error →
Check SMTP relay reachability.
Check service account authentication credentials.
Validate TLS configuration.
• No error message, but no delivery →
Check mail queue.
Check spam/filter rules.

Ticket number: TI-1571046

Contact person: Contact Person 05
Owner: Employee 21
Origin: Portal
Type: Incident
Created on: 04.03.25 13:18
Closure
Closure date: 11.03.25 16:02
Closure text: Root cause identified (Token caching & Conditional Access evaluation delay). Access stable after policy adjustment.
Activities
04.03.25 13:18
Ticket created via customer portal
Description:
Several employees report that after being added to the project “ERP-Upgrade”, they generally have access to SharePoint, but the folder
/Projects/ERP_Upgrade/Finance
is not displayed.
In some cases, access works for certain users after logging out and back in, but not for others.
Error message:
Access denied or item not found
04.03.25 13:32
Ticket assigned to: Employee 21
Status: In Progress
Notes:
04.03.25 14:05 – Initial analysis
• Affected users are members of Azure AD group “ERP_Finance”
• This group is a member of “SP_ERP_Project”
• SharePoint permissions correctly configured
• Comparison with working user shows identical group structure
Test:
Direct access via URL → partially successful
Notable:
Users with activated MFA requirement report issues more frequently.
04.03.25 15:40 – Conditional Access review
Tenant uses Conditional Access Policy:
“Require compliant device for Finance Resources”
Policy was adjusted on 28.02.
Some users access from private devices.
Device status in Entra ID partially “unknown”.
05.03.25 09:10 – Token analysis
Azure Sign-In Logs reviewed:
• Multiple entries with “Conditional Access not satisfied”
• At the same time, successful token issuance for SharePoint Root
Hypothesis:
Token was issued before group change → contains outdated claims
Users who had not fully logged out were working with cached tokens.
05.03.25 10:25 – Test measure
• User fully logged out (Office + browser)
• Browser cache cleared
• Device compliance status checked
Result:
Access possible afterward.
06.03.25 11:50 – In-depth analysis
Issue reoccurs for 2 users.
Difference identified:
These users access via Hybrid Azure AD Joined Devices.
Device synchronization was faulty.
Device compliance status was only updated after 6–8 hours.
07.03.25 09:30 – Actions
• Conditional Access Policy adjusted:
→ SharePoint project folder no longer included in Finance-specific CA rule
• Users re-authenticated
• Device sync triggered manually
10.03.25 – Monitoring
No further access issues reported.
Sign-In Logs unremarkable.
11.03.25 16:02 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 05
The root cause was not group nesting, but a delay in Conditional Access policy evaluation combined with cached authentication tokens.
After policy adjustment and renewed authentication, access is now stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

Access denied or item not found

04.03.25 14:05 – Initial analysis

Tenant uses Conditional Access Policy:
“Require compliant device for Finance Resources”
Policy was adjusted on 28.02.
Some users access from private devices.
Device status in Entra ID partially “unknown”.

Azure Sign-In Logs reviewed:
• Multiple entries with “Conditional Access not satisfied”
• At the same time, successful token issuance for SharePoint Root
Hypothesis:
Token was issued before group change → contains outdated claims
Users who had not fully logged out were working with cached tokens.
05.03.25 10:25 – Test measure
• User fully logged out (Office + browser)
• Browser cache cleared
• Device compliance status checked
Result:
Access possible afterward.
06.03.25 11:50 – In-depth analysis

These users access via Hybrid Azure AD Joined Devices.

• Users re-authenticated

Sign-In Logs unremarkable.

The root cause was not group nesting, but a delay in Conditional Access policy evaluation combined with cached authentication tokens.
After policy adjustment and renewed authentication, access is now stable.

04.03.25 13:18
Ticket created via customer portal

Several employees report that after being added to the project “ERP-Upgrade”, they generally have access to SharePoint, but the folder
/Projects/ERP_Upgrade/Finance
is not displayed.

Error message:
Access denied or item not found
04.03.25 13:32
Ticket assigned to: Employee 21
Status: In Progress
Notes:
04.03.25 14:05 – Initial analysis
• Affected users are members of Azure AD group “ERP_Finance”
• This group is a member of “SP_ERP_Project”
• SharePoint permissions correctly configured
• Comparison with working user shows identical group structure
Test:
Direct access via URL → partially successful
Notable:
Users with activated MFA requirement report issues more frequently.
04.03.25 15:40 – Conditional Access review
Tenant uses Conditional Access Policy:
“Require compliant device for Finance Resources”

• User fully logged out (Office + browser)
• Browser cache cleared
• Device compliance status checked
Result:
Access possible afterward.
06.03.25 11:50 – In-depth analysis

These users access via Hybrid Azure AD Joined Devices.

Sign-In Logs unremarkable.
11.03.25 16:02 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 05
The root cause was not group nesting, but a delay in Conditional Access policy evaluation combined with cached authentication tokens.
After policy adjustment and renewed authentication, access is now stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

In-depth analysis
10.04.25 09:40
Azure AD Connect reviewed.
Observation:

10.04.25 11:25

→ Contains external guests
Synchronized On-Prem group:
→ Contains only internal users

11.04.25 08:30

• On-Prem group re-synchronized
• Cloud group overwritten
• External users reproducibly lose access
Root Cause
Two groups existed with identical name:
COMPLIANCE_TEAM
1. Cloud-native group
2. Synchronized On-Prem group
Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.
SharePoint referenced the synchronized version.
External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group.
Actions
11.04.25 – 14.04.25

• Old Cloud group removed
• On-Prem group properly cleaned

The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized).

For two affected users in Entra ID portal, attribute
“source” = Windows Server AD
For functioning external users:
“source” = Azure AD

09.04.25 14:20
Ticket escalated for in-depth analysis.
Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – Identity
10.04.25 08:55
Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 14
In-depth analysis
10.04.25 09:40
Azure AD Connect reviewed.
Observation:
Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud.
On-Prem version is synchronized.
Cloud version was originally created manually.
Result:
• External guests exist only in Cloud group
• SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group
Same name, but different Object IDs.
10.04.25 11:25
Review of group members via Graph:
Cloud group:
→ Contains external guests
Synchronized On-Prem group:
→ Contains only internal users
Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect.
11.04.25 08:30
Reproduction in test:
• On-Prem group re-synchronized
• Cloud group overwritten
• External users reproducibly lose access
Root Cause
Two groups existed with identical name:
COMPLIANCE_TEAM
1. Cloud-native group
2. Synchronized On-Prem group
Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.
SharePoint referenced the synchronized version.
External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group.
Actions
11.04.25 – 14.04.25
• Consolidation of group structure
• Old Cloud group removed
• On-Prem group properly cleaned
• External guests managed as Cloud-only members
• Azure AD Connect full synchronization performed
15.04.25 – Monitoring
Access remains stable
No further inconsistencies
17.04.25 15:18 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 07
The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized).
Due to synchronization, effective group membership for external users was not correctly applied.
The group structure was consolidated and fully synchronized.
Access is now stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

=== CITED COMMUNITY REPORTS ===
REPORT 109 [resolved-as: community_report]:
# CheckPoint IKEv2 VPN Failures — PKI Template EKU Omission

A weekend PKI/template refresh produced a refreshed certificate template that omitted the "IP Security IKE intermediate" EKU, causing server-side VPN certificates to lack the EKU required by the CheckPoint VPN concentrator for IKEv2 mutual authentication. As a result, IKEv2 negotiations failed with client certificate chain validation errors across heterogeneous remote client OSs and impacted ~80 VPN users. Troubleshooting used CheckPoint IKEv2 logs that showed "EKU mismatch" and "client cert chain validation failed" entries. The internal PKI reissued corrected client certificates from a corrected template and deployed them to endpoints via Group Policy to remediate the incident and restore connectivity; the ticket closure documents the reissuance and deployment activity and user validation.

## Root cause: refreshed certificate template omitted the IPsec IKE EKU

The incident root cause was a refreshed certificate template that removed the IP Security IKE intermediate EKU, leaving only the Server Authentication EKU on new certificates; server-side VPN certificates issued from that template therefore did not present the IKE EKU expected by the VPN concentrator. This template refresh was performed by the internal PKI during weekend maintenance and is identified as the origin of the incompatibility. [Data: Entities (1474, 1477, 1475); Relationships (2936, 2941)]

## Protocol-level failure: CheckPoint cluster rejected certificate chain (EKU mismatch)

The CheckPoint VPN concentrator performed certificate validations during IKEv2 negotiations and logged explicit 'EKU mismatch' and 'client cert chain validation failed' errors that pointed to the missing IPsec IKE EKU on the presented certificate chain. These IKEv2 negotiation logs were the primary diagnostic artifact used to identify that the certificate template change caused the validation failures. [Data: Entities (1478, 1482, 1477); Relationships (2948, 2946)]

## Scope and user impact: ~80 remote/home VPN users experienced authentication failures with varying client errors

Approximately eighty remote/home VPN clients attempted IKEv2 connections with mutual certificate authentication and experienced failures; depending on the client OS, users saw differing messages such as 'authentication failed' or 'could not validate server certificate'. The incident therefore had a moderate but meaningful user impact across heterogeneous endpoint platforms. [Data: Entities (1480, 1481); Relationships (2951, 2952)]

## Certificate chain detail: server-side cert issued from refreshed template lacked required EKU

Server-side VPN certificate(s) were issued from the refreshed template and consequently did not contain the IP Security IKE intermediate EKU required by CheckPoint for IKEv2 mutual auth; the CheckPoint cluster's validation of that server certificate triggered the IKE negotiation failures. This explains why TLS/general server authentication would still work for many services (Server Authentication EKU present) while IKEv2-specific validation failed. [Data: Entities (1475, 1474, 235); Relationships (2938, 2942)]

## Remediation performed: corrected template, reissued client certificates, deployed via GPO and validated

The remediation path documented in the ticket included producing a corrected certificate template that restored the required EKU set, reissuing client/user certificates from that corrected template, and deploying the corrected client certificates to affected endpoints using Group Policy; the ticket closure notes that reissued client certificates were deployed and validated by users. These actions resolved the certificate EKU mismatch and restored IKEv2 connectivity. [Data: Entities (1485, 1472, 1476, 1484); Relationships (2927, 2956, 2944)]

## SCEP-issued user certificates state during the event and remediation

User/client certificates that were originally issued via SCEP were reported by the PKI team as not having been reissued or expired during the weekend maintenance and therefore remained unchanged at the time the template refresh caused the server-side incompatibility; remediation included reissuing client certs as part of the fix. This clarifies that both server-side template change and subsequent client-side reissuance were part of the incident lifecycle. [Data: Entities (1476); Relationships (2944)]

## Operational risk: PKI/template changes without end-to-end validation create service-impacting regressions

This incident demonstrates a systemic operational risk: changes to certificate templates (EKU composition) made in the PKI affected downstream consumers (IKEv2 on CheckPoint, legacy LDAP-S clients that expect Kerberos EKU) and produced a production-impacting outage for remote VPN users. The refreshed template replaced the previous template and omitted the IKE EKU that had been present before, causing incompatibility with existing consumers. Absent pre-deployment validation of template EKU effects against representative relying parties, such PKI changes can cause broad service disruption. [Data: Entities (1474, 1477); Relationships (2941, 2936, 430)]

## Diagnostics and observability: IKEv2 logs were decisive for root-cause identification

IKEv2 negotiation logs from the CheckPoint cluster contained explicit EKU mismatch and certificate chain validation failure entries that directly pointed investigators to the template EKU omission and enabled targeted remediation (template correction and certificate reissuance). These logs therefore proved critical in the troubleshooting path. [Data: Entities (1482, 1478); Relationships (2948, 2946, 2955)]

## Heterogeneous client behavior complicates triage and user communication

Client endpoints running different OSs produced different error messages (for example, 'authentication failed' vs 'could not validate server certificate'), which complicates initial triage and user guidance because symptoms varied across the affected population even though the underlying cause was identical (EKU mismatch). Correlating IKE logs with client reports was necessary to unify the diagnosis. [Data: Entities (1481, 1486); Relationships (2952, 2955)]

## Recommended prioritized actions to reduce recurrence (operational recommendations)

Based on the documented cause and remediation, prioritized recommendations are: 1) enforce PKI change-control and a pre-deployment validation checklist that explicitly verifies EKU sets required by known relying parties (e.g., CheckPoint IKE, legacy LDAP clients); 2) stage template changes in a non-production environment and run end-to-end IKE/LDAP validations against representative devices and CheckPoint instances before wide issuance; 3) add automated EKU/compliance checks and certificate chain validation tests as part of PKI template CI/CD; 4) maintain a map of certificate consumers (which templates are used by which services) and require service-owner signoff for template changes; and 5) improve monitoring and alerting on IKEv2 negotiation failures (surface EKU mismatch events to PKI/Network ops). These recommendations are analyst actions derived from the incident facts and remediation path (corrected template and GPO deployment) rather than new claims about the event. [Data: Entities (1485, 1472); Relationships (2927, 2956)]

[Data: Entities (1478, 1482, 1477); Relationships (2948, 2946)]

## Scope and user impact: ~80 remote/home VPN users experienced authentication failures with varying client errors

Approximately eighty remote/home VPN clients attempted IKEv2 connections with mutual certificate authentication and experienced failures; depending on the client OS, users saw differing messages such as 'authentication failed' or 'could not validate server certificate'. The incident therefore had a moderate but meaningful user impact across heterogeneous endpoint platforms. [Data: Entities (1480, 1481); Relationships (2951, 2952)]

## Certificate chain detail: server-side cert issued from refreshed template lacked required EKU

Server-side VPN certificate(s) were issued from the refreshed template and consequently did not contain the IP Security IKE intermediate EKU required by CheckPoint for IKEv2 mutual auth; the CheckPoint cluster's validation of that server certificate triggered the IKE negotiation failures. This explains why TLS/general server authentication would still work for many services (Server Authentication EKU present) while IKEv2-specific validation failed. [Data: Entities (1475, 1474, 235); Relationships (2938, 2942)]

## Remediation performed: corrected template, reissued client certificates, deployed via GPO and validated

The remediation path documented in the ticket included producing a corrected certificate template that restored the required EKU set, reissuing client/user certificates from that corrected template, and deploying the corrected client certificates to affected endpoints using Group Policy; the ticket closure notes that reissued client certificates were deployed and validated by users. These actions resolved the certificate EKU mismatch and restored IKEv2 connectivity. [Data: Entities (1485, 1472, 1476, 1484); Relationships (2927, 2956, 2944)]

## SCEP-issued user certificates state during the event and remediation

User/client certificates that were originally issued via SCEP were reported by the PKI team as not having been reissued or expired during the weekend maintenance and therefore remained unchanged at the time the template refresh caused the server-side incompatibility; remediation included reissuing client certs as part of the fix. This clarifies that both server-side template change and subsequent client-side reissuance were part of the incident lifecycle. [Data: Entities (1476); Relationships (2944)]

## Operational risk: PKI/template changes without end-to-end validation create service-impacting regressions

This incident demonstrates a systemic operational risk: changes to certificate templates (EKU composition) made in the PKI affected downstream consumers (IKEv2 on CheckPoint, legacy LDAP-S clients that expect Kerberos EKU) and produced a production-impacting outage for remote VPN users. The refreshed template replaced the previous template and omitted the IKE EKU that had been present before, causing incompatibility with existing consumers. Absent pre-deployment validation of template EKU effects against representative relying parties, such PKI changes can cause broad service disruption. [Data: Entities (1474, 1477); Relationships (2941, 2936, 430)]

## Diagnostics and observability: IKEv2 logs were decisive for root-cause identification

IKEv2 negotiation logs from the CheckPoint cluster contained explicit EKU mismatch and certificate chain validation failure entries that directly pointed investigators to the template EKU omission and enabled targeted remediation (template correction and certificate reissuance). These logs therefore proved critical in the troubleshooting path. [Data: Entities (1482, 1478); Relationships (2948, 2946, 2955)]

## Heterogeneous client behavior complicates triage and user communication

Client endpoints running different OSs produced different error messages (for example, 'authentication failed' vs 'could not validate server certificate'), which complicates initial triage and user guidance because symptoms varied across the affected population even though the underlying cause was identical (EKU mismatch). Correlating IKE logs with client reports was necessary to unify the diagnosis. [Data: Entities (1481, 1486); Relationships (2952, 2955)]

## Recommended prioritized actions to reduce recurrence (operational recommendations)

Based on the documented cause and remediation, prioritized recommendations are: 1) enforce PKI change-control and a pre-deployment validation checklist that explicitly verifies EKU sets required by known relying parties (e.g., CheckPoint IKE, legacy LDAP clients); 2) stage template changes in a non-production environment and run end-to-end IKE/LDAP validations against representative devices and CheckPoint instances before wide issuance; 3) add automated EKU/compliance checks and certificate chain validation tests as part of PKI template CI/CD; 4) maintain a map of certificate consumers (which templates are used by which services) and require service-owner signoff for template changes; and 5) improve monitoring and alerting on IKEv2 negotiation failures (surface EKU mismatch events to PKI/Network ops). These recommendations are analyst actions derived from the incident facts and remediation path (corrected template and GPO deployment) rather than new claims about the event. [Data: Entities (1485, 1472); Relationships (2927, 2956)]

REPORT 104 [resolved-as: community_report]:
# PKI Distribution Failure: KF80-ISSUING-CA-01 → AIA Share → Service Certificate Validation

A recently renewed intermediate CA (KF80-ISSUING-CA-01) had its new issuing certificate installed on the CA server but the PKI team did not publish the renewed intermediate to the configured internal AIA/CRL distribution points. Service certificates issued after the renewal embedded AIA/CRL URLs that pointed to the new intermediate location, and client machines failed certificate chain validation until the intermediate and its CRL were published and clients refreshed their intermediate stores (via normal refresh cycles or forced gpupdate / certutil -pulse). The incident was diagnosed as a PKI distribution-layer problem (AIA/CRL publishing and file-share publication) rather than a per-service certificate configuration error; remediation included publishing the intermediate and CRLs to the AIA/CRL distribution points and coordinating client-side certificate-store refresh actions to cache the new issuing certificate.

## Root cause — renewed issuing CA cert was not published to the AIA/CRL distribution points

The issuing (intermediate) CA was renewed and the new intermediate certificate was installed on the CA server, but the renewed intermediate was not published to the internal AIA/CRL distribution points; this gap prevented clients from retrieving the intermediate during chain building. This behavior and cause are explicitly described for KF80-ISSUING-CA-01 and the NEW ISSUING CA CERT in the incident notes [Data: Entities (815, 831); Relationships (1583)].

## Client-side validation failures for service certificates issued after the renewal

Service certificates issued after the CA renewal contained AIA URLs referencing the new issuing/ intermediate certificate location, so client chain validation failed when the intermediate was unavailable locally or on the AIA share. This directly impacted service validation—examples include the HR portal whose TLS certificate was signed by KF80-ISSUING-CA-01—and client attempts to validate those certificates failed until the intermediate was retrievable [Data: Entities (836, 815); Relationships (1592, 1557)].

## PKI distribution-layer and AIA share (file server) were the operational fault lines

The incident was diagnosed as a PKI distribution-layer issue rather than a leaf/service certificate misconfiguration, pointing to gaps in the process that publishes CA artifacts to distribution points and the file/HTTP origin that serves them. The AIA distribution point share is implemented on a FILE SHARE SERVER and is part of the PKI DISTRIBUTION LAYER, and the missing publication meant the file share did not contain the new issuing certificate for client retrieval [Data: Entities (842, 833, 844); Relationships (1589, 1590)].

## Remediation required both server-side publication and client-side refresh actions

Resolution required publishing the NEW ISSUING CA CERT and its CRL to the AIA/CRL distribution locations and then accelerating client cache updates. Closure notes indicate the intermediate and CRL were published to the AIA/CRL distribution points and clients were verified to validate affected services thereafter; operators also performed coordinated client refresh actions (gpupdate /force; certutil -pulse) on sample machines to confirm caching of the new intermediate [Data: Entities (816, 840); Relationships (1562, 1604)].

## Client caching behavior and timeframe produced a risk window

Clients follow embedded AIA URLs to download missing intermediate certificates and store them in their INTERMEDIATE CA STORE; without a forced refresh, typical refresh cycles meant some clients would not obtain the intermediate for hours. The remediation notes describe clients picking up the newly published issuing CA cert within normal refresh windows (2–4 hours) and faster when gpupdate / certutil refreshes were applied to sample CLIENT MACHINES [Data: Entities (843, 839, 840); Relationships (1602, 1604)].

## Existing certificates chained to the old issuing CA limited immediate impact but created future risk

Service certificates issued prior to the renewal continued to chain to the OLD ISSUING CA CERT and therefore did not show immediate failures; however, certificates issued after renewal relied on the new intermediate and thus exposed reliance on timely AIA publication. This differentiation explains why some services continued to function while newly issued certs failed until publishing was completed [Data: Entities (832, 836); Relationships (1585)].

## Diagnostics and evidence were available via browser inspection and chain diagnostics

Operators used browser certificate inspection to identify that the chain broke at the issuing CA because the intermediate was missing from the client view; the CERTIFICATE CHAIN diagnostic and browser inspection UI were used to capture the missing issuer evidence during troubleshooting. This provided clear, actionable diagnostic evidence of the missing intermediate in client chains [Data: Entities (826); Relationships (1573)].

## Recommended mitigations: procedural controls, automation, monitoring, and runbook additions

To prevent recurrence, introduce mandatory, automated AIA/CRL publication steps in the CA renewal process (ensure NEW ISSUING CA CERT and CRL are pushed to the AIA distribution share and verified), add post-renewal validation checks that follow the AIA URL to confirm availability, and create an incident runbook that includes steps to force client cache refresh (gpupdate / certutil -pulse) and validate client INTERMEDIATE CA STORE state. The incident evidence identifies the relevant artifacts and interfaces to be included in those controls (KF80-ISSUING-CA-01, NEW ISSUING CA CERT, AIA DISTRIBUTION POINT SHARE, PKI DISTRIBUTION LAYER) and supports automating verification of AIA URL resolution and file share publication as part of CA renewals [Data: Entities (815, 831, 833, 842); Relationships (1586, 1589)].

The remediation notes describe clients picking up the newly published issuing CA cert within normal refresh windows (2–4 hours) and faster when gpupdate / certutil refreshes were applied to sample CLIENT MACHINES [Data: Entities (843, 839, 840); Relationships (1602, 1604)].

## Existing certificates chained to the old issuing CA limited immediate impact but created future risk

Service certificates issued prior to the renewal continued to chain to the OLD ISSUING CA CERT and therefore did not show immediate failures; however, certificates issued after renewal relied on the new intermediate and thus exposed reliance on timely AIA publication. This differentiation explains why some services continued to function while newly issued certs failed until publishing was completed [Data: Entities (832, 836); Relationships (1585)].

## Diagnostics and evidence were available via browser inspection and chain diagnostics

Operators used browser certificate inspection to identify that the chain broke at the issuing CA because the intermediate was missing from the client view; the CERTIFICATE CHAIN diagnostic and browser inspection UI were used to capture the missing issuer evidence during troubleshooting. This provided clear, actionable diagnostic evidence of the missing intermediate in client chains [Data: Entities (826); Relationships (1573)].

## Recommended mitigations: procedural controls, automation, monitoring, and runbook additions

To prevent recurrence, introduce mandatory, automated AIA/CRL publication steps in the CA renewal process (ensure NEW ISSUING CA CERT and CRL are pushed to the AIA distribution share and verified), add post-renewal validation checks that follow the AIA URL to confirm availability, and create an incident runbook that includes steps to force client cache refresh (gpupdate / certutil -pulse) and validate client INTERMEDIATE CA STORE state. The incident evidence identifies the relevant artifacts and interfaces to be included in those controls (KF80-ISSUING-CA-01, NEW ISSUING CA CERT, AIA DISTRIBUTION POINT SHARE, PKI DISTRIBUTION LAYER) and supports automating verification of AIA URL resolution and file share publication as part of CA renewals [Data: Entities (815, 831, 833, 842); Relationships (1586, 1589)].
